Hey guys, Jordan Belfort here. Time for your weekly intensive, but this is round two. So for those of you who were on the actual live stream yesterday, you know that we had some technical difficulties. And what happened was they kept recording and kept, it was live stream, kept cutting out every 10 minutes or so, every five minutes or so. And it got to the point where it was sort of diminishing returns. We switched locations, taking that. The problem was on our end here, there was a bad connection. Turned out it was an issue on Facebook yesterday. So not only did it keep cutting out, but it actually erased our data. So the way it typically works is I'll do my live stream on Saturday morning, 11 o'clock, right? And then maybe 10, 20% of the people on the program watch it live, and the rest watch it over the next few days, right? And some watch it over the next couple of weeks. So we then export it from Facebook onto our platform. Well, for some reason, Facebook was having this problem not just with us yesterday, but around the world. People were losing data, so couldn't get my stuff out of Facebook. So what happened was now is I have 30 minutes worth of this training that was recorded yesterday. That I have. That's the last 30 minutes. What I don't have is the first 30 minutes of the training. So I'm going to do it again now. So you notice I'm wearing a different shirt, the location's different, so I'm going to, and by the way, I think this will be even better than yesterday's initial 30 minutes, because now I have time to think about what I said, sort of streamlined it a bit, right? So let's rock and roll here. And let me stop by saying I want to wish you all a really merry Christmas, happy Hanukkah, and merry Kwanzaa, whatever your religion is. And also, if I don't speak to you before New Year's Eve, happy New Year, right? It's been a great year. And I keep saying to everyone that this is our year. 2019 is going to be that year where you make big money. And the problem with those sort of statements is they are so cliche, this is when they slip up everyone's tongue, next year is going to be all year. It's almost like things like that lose their power. And I always talk about losing the power. What I mean by that is that it's when something that was actually a really powerful motivating force, when it was first said, when the concept was first realized by people and spread around the self-development world, it worked. People got good results. But then over time, things have a way of getting deluded and bastardized. And they almost become buzzwords or cliches. And they lose their power. And they no longer help you achieve success. Perfect example of that is goal setting. I'm a goal-oriented person. You've heard me say many times that goals by themselves really almost have become so bastardized. Like everyone's like, I'm a goal-oriented person. I'm a goal-centered. That it loses its power. Because people are looking to get set a goal. You don't achieve a goal. Who gives a shit? You don't really set the goal the right way anyway. It's not related to a vision on top. So I have a way of, you know, I sort of talk down about goals. But here's the rub. I am a serial goal-centered. I set goals all the time in every aspect of my business and my life in that matter. But mostly business, because I'm more regimented there. But I do set goals in my personal life as well. Here's the difference. There is a way to set goals that allows you to be incredibly effective, to tip the odds of success dramatically in your favor, and to really help you get what you want in any aspect of your life. Goal setting is fucking powerful. But you've got to do it a certain way. So the reason I'm going to discuss it today is because it's the end of the year. And the purpose really is to explain to you why I really know in my heart of hearts. Why I'm not just giving you lip service. So this is going to be 2019, your year. As long as you stick with this and stay true to the program, which is about growing yourself, about essentially getting rich in the real way. Meaning, like, you know, all this, there's so much nonsense online and so many, you know, free webinars and turnkey solutions for getting rich. And they're bullshit. They don't work. And the reason they don't work is that the one surefire way of getting rich out there that really does work is to turn yourself into a force of nature. Is to learn all the skills, meet the people that you need to know, have all the connections that are going to share your ideas. It's, listen, it's about you. And I've said this many times, but I need to beat this dead horse a bit more here because the subject of this training here is going to be how you set goals correctly, how you link them up to your vision, and how you use that to figure out what things do you need to know? What skill sets do you need to acquire to become successful? In other words, people will set a goal, right? And usually the goal they set is wrong anyway. Like, it's not stretching them enough. We'll get to that later. But to set a goal in a vacuum is just patently ridiculous. In other words, just I'm going to do X number. I'm going to get X number of leads in one week. I'm going to make X number of dials per day. Just if you set a goal like that, I promise you, you're not in for a life of wealth, success, and happiness. You're in for a life of cereal, fucking, chasing after your goals and kind of feeling shitty all the time. I can achieve it. Who's like, you're trying to make money. You probably do OK, but you never really get to that higher level where you really make a lot of money and you feel good about it. And that's the result of setting goals incorrectly. When I talk about setting goals, what I mean is creating a roadmap to your vision for the future. Now, I want you to watch this here. So we're starting from scratch here from yesterday, right? So I'm going to teach you what's really an eight or 10-step process here, depending on what happens when you actually execute the left of your goal. But watch what happens here. So this is your vision for the future, right? This is where you want to go in your three to five years. Now, where do you see yourself and why, right? That's an emotionally charged vision of where you really see your life in all aspects. And this is the target that you aim for. It's what you're aiming for. It gives you that precise thing. This is what I want to end up. And that alerts a certain part of your brain to say, well, OK, since I want to end up there, what should be important to me? And what can I ignore? Because there's so much shit coming at us in the form of just outside stimuli opportunities, lessons to learn, skills to possess. How do you know what to possess, what not to possess, what to learn, what not to learn? How do you know what you should ignore, what to go after? You don't, right? The vision, not only does it give you a target, like a clear target to aim for, but it gives you context of how to manage your time, of how to go about building up yourself. Meaning how do you have to change yourself to get where you want to go? The vision is the basis for everything. And you want to be emotionally attached to it, meaning it's your why. At the highest level, why is this so important to you? It's about your children, your wife, your family, your husband, the community you live in, the causes you believe in. It's about your core beliefs, your values, very deep. You all know the story when I was in jail, my why, my WHY, or my vision was my children, that I could get out of jail, make my kids proud. And that was a power that allowed me to, inner power, to overcome any obstacle standing in my way. Not in the bullshit cliched sort of self-development. We say, you know, there's a force inside of you. And once you unleash that force, you can accomplish anything you've probably heard that nonsense, right, from someone else. Here's the thing, it's nonsense because they don't really ever show you how to do it. What really is required to unleash this power within you? When you know how to unleash the power, which starts, by the way, with the voice in your own head, your own mindset, what, you know, is it fear-based? Or are you basically, you know, moving, but playing offense or defense essentially, right? But let me just get back to this understanding here of your vision, right? And this is where you are today. This is your starting point, all right? Then you have these sort of stopping off points, these decision trees, these different testing, you know, methodology you'll have all the way up to your vision. It'll be nice to have a straight line to your vision, but it's just not the way it works. You're not going to have a straight line to success. You're going to be wrong a lot more than you're right. But as I always say, my great metaphor, my favorite one is, it's like about, you know, it's about like, you know, cops and robbers, the police, the detectives. They only have to be right once. A criminal's got to be right every single time. One mistake, there's death toast. Well, guess what? With success, you only have to be right once and you're rich. So on some level, achieving success is about learning how to take massive action and being wrong and learning how to live to fight another day. To not blow out, to not blow yourself up, to not get saddled with limiting beliefs and essentially maximize on the lessons from failure, because that's where the lessons are in the failures, right? Minimize on the pain, the financials, the time you lose. But you're getting these lessons learned. You're growing yourself. You're changing your approach. And then eventually by doing that, you go to your vision. Now that, my friends, is not a straight line. That's a kind of, you know, a circuitous path we take. We take three steps forward, two steps backwards. That's the way it is. Bottom line, you get used to it. So just remember, your context for 2019 is that you are not going to be right most of the time in business. And when I say in business, I mean, if you're in sales, you are a business owner. You have to start thinking of yourself like, I own my own company. I'm growing my own business within someone else's business. I just finished a consulting job for a few days, right? And I will tell you, big company in logistics, right? And the one single thing, the manager was, the owner was desperately trying to instill in his sales force, is that you guys are in your own businesses. Think like an entrepreneur. And watch what happens. Invest in yourself. Invest in your every business owner wants their salespeople to think that way. So it's not contrary to like, you know, if you're a work person, you should quit your job. No, grow within the company, OK, if you're in a good company. So we have the vision. We have our starting point. And then we have our goals. We're going to have daily goals. We're going to have weekly goals. We're going to have monthly goals. We could have quarterly goals. And we have annual goals, all right? And we can have, you know, two year and three year goals. But once you get past annual goals, you know, it starts to get a lot more fuzzy because it's very hard to project out that long. Unless you're a really big established company, then it gets a bit easy. But the point is we have all these different types of goals. And here's the thing. I am, again, very pro goal setting. The problem is, is that 99% of the people who set goals are doing it incorrectly. So what I'm going to do now for the rest of this training is I want to give you, I'm going to hand you essentially a roadmap to this powerful methodology for setting goals correctly. And it allows you to link them up to your vision and basically get the result you want every single time, eventually. Because remember, failure is part of the problem. Now, let's go back in history a bit to 1959, 1960. There was a record, a vinyl record, before you guys don't even know what they are, right? Released by someone named Earl Nightingale. And it was called The Strangest Secret. And The Strangest Secret, and he had this booming voice, Earl Nightingale, he talked about, you know, we are what we think about. And the crux of it was that you need to set goals because they did a study, I think it was either Harvard or Yale, but of the graduating class of the 5% of the people who set goals in the graduating class of 1960, 1950, whatever it was, right? They ended up making more money than the other 95% of the students who did the set goals. And this study spurred us revolution of goal setting and becoming a goal-oriented society. Now, I seem to remember that this study was actually debunked. Like, it wasn't true. What happened? Like, it wasn't, it didn't really happen. The people from the university didn't set goals in Apple form. But the point was made. And it went, wow, The Strangest Secret became a huge hit. It sold over a million copies. And we became a goal-oriented society. By the time I was in going to college, goals were already a big buzzword. And, you know, we're setting goals. And, you know, and it was like, you heard about it even in movies. They were talking about, you know, it was a new thing. It was a thing about being sort of, you know, a success-oriented person. The seminar was growing and blossoming. NLP was forming, right? And goals became all the rage. And they worked. In the beginning, they worked as people took it seriously. People would set goals and they would, you know, stick to them and really try to achieve them. And then over time, as most things essentially do, they become cliched and they lose their power. People stop taking them seriously. The context gets lost. The message gets diluted down by, you know, the 24th or 25th person now, teaching that message, sticking them to telephoto. It changes a little bit each time. And before you know it, it's bullshit. People set goals. They abandon their goals and they say, who gives a fuck? And they don't matter. That's why I, when I first went out into the self-development world, I saw what was going on with those goal-setting bullshit. I'm like, guys, it's about vision. You have to have a vision on top of your goals. And yes, I do set goals, but the vision that essentially serves as your target to aim for and your goals serve as your signposts along the way. In other words, your goals are there. And again, with still things I've said before, now we're starting about to dig very deep here, right? So goals are your signposts. They tell you if you're going in the right direction or the wrong direction. Am I failing? Am I succeeding? Am I moving towards my vision or away from my vision? Do I need to change my approach or is my approach exactly right? That's what goals are designed for. And the reason that you don't want to become emotionally attached to them is because when you're setting your goals correctly, you're mostly not hitting them. You're falling short. In other words, when we set a goal, the first key here is that you need to stretch yourself. OK? There's a sweet spot of setting a goal. Let me give you an example. I want to have that by the end of 2019, I would like to have one million subscribers on my platform. That's what I want. I want to have one million subscribers, pay me between $10,000 and $15,000 a month. That's my goal. And I know that that goal is achievable. A lot of things have to go right. I have to align myself with a lot of people, the certain things I have to still learn. A lot has to happen, but it's an achievable goal. Am I going to get there? Probably not. I'm telling you, it's not that I'm saying I definitely won't, but it's going to be a fucking stretch and a half. I would like, here's what I'd like to happen. I'd like to shoot for a million and end up at $500,000 and fail and do half as well. And that's a pretty fucking amazing business. In other words, when I set a goal, I stretch it out to a level like I probably won't get there. Everything's got to go right. And by the way, everyone's like, I'll blow away a goal. You know, you just get lucky and the stars line, you can blow it away. But I'll set my goal to try to like, you know, almost like maybe 70%, let's say 50, 60% above where I know I could comfortably hit. And the problem with most people though is they set that goal. They think they should set that goal where they think they can hit it. And that's ridiculous because here's what happens. That's being average. That's average performance. Or I think I can go, it's my average win. You know, if things go, okay, that's what I'll do. That's not a stretch. That's why I always say the enemy of great is good. When you are feeling good about things, that's okay, I'm average. There's no pain to change. So what happens if you want to be extraordinary, all right, then what's going to draw you there is partially the pain of failure. If you're just feeling I'm okay, if I'm good, there's no impetus to change. Change when you're ready happens in a heartbeat, happens in an instant. What takes a long time is all the shit you've got to go through, all the pain, the suffering, or before you're so fed up that you finally make the change. When you make the change, bam, it happens in a heartbeat. Same thing with wealth and success. You know, the only way to get rich is quick. Bam, when you get rich, it happens quick. What takes a long time is all the stuff you've got to go through before all the elements of success are lined up and you start getting rich quick. So there's like a process to go through. But when it finally happens, it happens like that, okay? So what happens here now with goals is because most people don't set their goals correctly and don't understand what they really mean. They don't serve their purpose, which is a direct link up, sort of a way for you to, this is too far out, it's five years okay. You know, how do you really, you know, that's where your emotional attachment has to be, is in your vision. But how do you really use that, so to speak, you know, as a practical day in, day out guidebook to getting what you want? Because so much shit's got to happen from here to here. Let me just tell you, fly it out, and here's the number one key, is that you are not going to be the same person that you are right now in three to five years from now when you've achieved your vision. When you hit that vision, you'll have grown, you'll have evolved, you'll be a far more powerful, better version of yourself and a more empowered version of yourself. So it's like almost like how do you actually relate, like, to get that when you're over here? Well, that's what your goals do. Your goals give you these sort of temporary wins and losses to say, am I heading in the right direction or the wrong direction? That's why they're there. Now, again, we have daily goals, weekly goals, monthly goals, right, quarterly. Here's the difference. When we're talking about goals that are daily and even weekly, but mostly there's a little sweets by it, right? Daily goals are strictly productivity oriented. When you're analyzing the results, we'll get to this, what you do with goals, how you actually set goals, part of analyzing results, right? But when you are setting daily goals and you're analyzing results, don't read into strategy. Am I doing it? Is my strategy right? Is my strategy wrong? Am I making mistakes? That's not what it's about. Daily goals are more about productivity. Am I making enough dials? Am I knocking on the door, are I getting enough hits on my landing page? It's not about the actual end result because you know why? Because it's probably not statistically significant. In other words, with such a small data sample, you can very easily draw the wrong conclusion if you start looking at your daily goals as a way to start changing strategies. Don't do that. In other words, when you have a daily goal, it's more about how much of it, am I doing enough work? Am I hitting on enough doors? Do I have enough advertising budget out there? Am I making enough dials on the phone? Am I getting enough in-person appointment or something? Am I essentially doing it whatever I'm doing enough? It's about productivity. Do I need to ramp up my activity, ramp up my budget? It's not about analyzing the results and saying, oh shit, I'm not making enough goals. If something is wrong with my script, I have to learn how to sell. You can't possibly, you get it? You don't understand yet. There's not enough results. You need to have a longer, sort of, a more day. So data set that spans over five days a week, whatever, you can really get a statistically significant result. So I want you to look at your short-term goals as mostly measures of productivity. And again, we call these things KPIs, key performance indicators. So when you're creating your KPIs for daily and weekly goals, especially for daily goals, I want you to focus almost exclusively on productivity. How many calls am I making? How many doors am I knocking on? How many leads am I getting? How many impressions am I making? Whatever your API is, look at it from more of a productivity versus I need to change my approach. Your data set is too small. As we start moving to weekly and then monthly goals, it starts to be about, OK, here's my results. Yes, I got to look at productivity as well, but I should have really resolved that issue by my daily goals, see if my productivity is good, right? But now I say, OK, here's my result. What am I doing? Is it a result great? Is it OK? Is it big? In other words, a goal is my target, right? And I stretch myself. And my question is, did I hit my goal? Did I miss my goal? And by how much? And based on that, we start working backwards and what do we need to change? You get it? So don't do that with the promise. And I see people react to daily goals as if they mean anything. They're just about productivity. OK, just get that through your head. Very important. So that being said, as we move past our daily goals into weekly goals and monthly goals and so forth, right? Let's now get into the specific strategy. This is the most guys, I'm telling you, this is the most powerful goal spreading strategy I have ever seen. And I've basically taken this from many. I and I mean, I took this from a lot of different people. It's no one individual. And then I put my own stuff into it. I looked at all the stuff that was out there about goal setting. Because when I decided I wanted to really teach this to myself, I said, you know, let me see everything out there. Because there's a lot of stuff out there. And I chunked it up into what I thought was the best possible strategy. And then I put my own stuff in there that I, you know, from my own experience, right? And I put a straight line philosophy to it and boom, this is what I came with, straight line goal setting. OK, and it's powerful and it works. And it will, and it will change your life seriously. And you'll see why in a second because it just sort of it tips the odds in your favor so dramatically starts with number one, set a goal, very simple, right? What is your goal? OK. And again, we have daily goals. We have weekly goals, quarterly goals, annual and so forth, right? Step number two, OK? And let me, once again, remember the key to effective goal setting in terms of what is the goal is you want to stretch yourself. Don't make the goal so outlandish that your brain says, ah, that's it's bullshit. It'll never, it'll never work. That's another example of something losing its power. When I say it loses its power, meaning it no longer motivates you, it no longer affects you, it no longer serves the purpose that it was supposed to serve. So if you set an outlandish goal, and even here's an outlandish goal, next year I want to be bigger than Netflix. That's outlandish. Next year I want to disrupt Amazon. That's outlandish. Not going to happen. I could wish and dream and all I want, that's not going to happen. OK? I'm going to, next year I'm going to take over the entire self-sufficiency. Everyone else will be out of it. I'm going to be the only, everyone will come. The whole world is going to be on my platform. It's idiotic. It's not, it's not a rational goal. A rational goal is that if everything goes right, and I just, if I am just on target with everything, what will it look like? If it's just, if it's just unbelievably great, that's a rational goal. That's a stretch goal. When I say to people, you know, raise your hand as high as you can. Everyone goes like this, my shoulder's very. I said, well, no, you're a bit higher. Everyone's like, what the fuck? I said as high as you can. But it's always that stretch on top where you can stretch. So you want to stretch yourself. But don't stretch yourself to the point where the goal becomes a false goal because your brain will say, you know what, it's all bullshit and it loses its power. OK? And on the con, conversely, as I said before, the enemy of great is good. Average sucks. So if you set, if you set average goals, what do you do? Again, the problem with most people in this world who set goals is they, not they set their goals too high and miss them, is that they set their goals too low and hit them. You get it? They don't set their goals at a lofty enough level. So they hit their goal, big fucking deal. Their average, average sucks. So remember, it starts with setting the correct goal. That's only step one. The second step here is to put a date on it. Put a date on your goal. Every goal has a precise date. It has a starting point and it has an ending point. Unlike your vision, which is in three to five years, that's different. OK? This has got a defined point in time. By definition, the goal has got a beginning date and it has an ending date. Now, in reality, just so you know, I always set daily goals. When I have what I'm selling and I have a sales force, all right, or mark out, I always have a daily goal. I always then have a weekly goal. But less important than my weekly goal, my monthly goal is what really matters. I don't focus on the week performance as much because things can vary from week to week. I want to know what happened over a month. So for me, my daily is really important. And then my daily almost carries me through each week. You get it? I look at the end of the week, but it's not as significant to me. I want to know what happens each day. I want to know what happens each month. And most importantly, as time passes, I want to know about the same month last year. One of the most important, again, getting back to KPIs for a second, one of the most important KPIs in any industry is called, in the retail, they call it same store sales, meaning it's OK if a company grows by opening up lots of new stores. They go from half a billion a year to a billion a year by opening up twice as many stores. But what if every store opened for more than one year, has gone down in revenue 20%? Not a very good sign. That's a very important KPI. So what you want to do when you set goals is remember, it's not just a goal. It's not just setting a vacuum. It's that where were you last year at this time? Think about that while you're setting your goals and analyzing results. So this is step two, put a date on it. Now, step three is when we start to really depart from most goal-setting strategies. Here we identify the people, groups, organizations, and most importantly, the skill sets that we will need to achieve our goal. This is the biggest mistake people make. They set a goal and they don't actually then work backwards and say, OK, let me future pace and imagine what my company, what does my business look like? What does my own sales funnel look like? If you're a sales person, what does my, when I say your business, I mean as a top producer, what does my business itself look like when I've hit the skull? And say, in order to achieve it, what people do I need to know that I don't know right now? What groups, what companies do you align myself with? What organizations do I need to join venture with? You don't actually think of the goals strategically. Like what do I got to do? Like is there certain people have to hire, certain people have to join venture with a certain organization you have to join, become a member of? You don't ever future pace. You got future pace this shit out. Identify the people, the groups, the organizations that you need to identify, that you need to essentially align yourself with, have some sort of relationship with to get what you want in life. There's always going to be other people helping you get what you want. It's never a one person army. Doesn't work that way. And the most important one of all, by the way, I mean they're all important, this one skill sets. What do you need to know? What skills do you need to possess at a very high level and then just what skills you need basic knowledge of to get what you want in life? When you set a goal, you say, okay, if I achieve that goal and I can imagine myself in six months, what is my life? What have I learned from that? What would I need to know to make that happen? And fucking learn it. Grow yourself. This is why I always say that the key to the whole thing, what this whole program, this weekly intensive, these daily motivations, what they're really about, it's a never ending process of growing yourself, of making yourself more effective, honing your skills, helping you find people that make the right introductions, opening up your eyes to what success is about. And that means you've got to learn shit. You've got to grow yourself. In the absence of that, it's almost like the definition of insanity. I'm going to do the same thing over and over again, expect different results. No, we don't do that. You have to grow yourself. So if you set a six month goal or a 90 day goal, ask yourself this question. What people do I need to meet right now or over this period to make this happen? What companies do I have to have relationships with? What organizations? Any industry trade associations that can help me out? And most importantly, what do I myself have to learn or hire someone that knows that skill? You get it? Like even I'm going to learn it myself, what someone else is going to give it to me? This is the linchpin step that fucks everyone over. They set a goal. They don't actually think of it deliberately. They don't future pace and say, what do I need to know? What do I got to learn? What do I have to learn myself? Which groups? Whatever. You get it? This is the major fall down here. So I want you to take step three and really slow this down and torture yourself to really dig down deep and find out what you need here on step three. That's power. Because what happens is by doing that, you make the goal real in your own mind. You start saying, well, should I got everything I need to possess to achieve this goal? It makes it more real. And that's where the power lies when you think you can actually do it. So with that, let's move on to step four. Step four is to create a plan to create a plan and take massive action. Massive action. Okay. Create a plan and take massive action. Or you could say to take massive action using the best available plan. Now, here's the caveat. There are no perfect plans. This represents a starting point. You want to develop the best plan possible. And the way we do that is by modeling. We try modeling other people who are already successful. Think about the evolution of my business. This training right now. This training represents the evolution of myself going out and saying, okay, I want to go out there and really start spreading my wings and really making sure everyone in the world knows about the straight line. Everyone can benefit from the success because people get massive results. Right. Well, I started looking at different models. I looked at the free webinar models and this sucks. It's bogus. I'm not doing it. And then I said, wait, look at the Netflix model. That's interesting. This subscription side of the equation. And I took three or four different models and used it as my target. That was my starting point. And then from there, boom, I kept honing it and changing my approach to you until I got where I am right now, which is an unbelievable spot. And it's on massive numbers of subscribers every day. People loving the content where I'm over delivering value, but building a business in the process. You get it? Now. You will never be able to create a perfect plan because the plan gets perfected by failing, by trialing and failing. In other words, you, you, you take that, you take a shot. You analyze your results, which is the next step, which is where, by the way, you're going to switch to me in my other house now because, you know, this is where we got that portion tape. So this is going to a couple of another minute or so, you'll see me pop into a different, I'll be in a different yesterday shirt. Right. But the point is, is that you create this plan through the process of modeling. And you want to share that plan with your mentor. You look at your daily goal. You look at your weekly say, where do I stand? Did I hit my goal? Am I under my goal? What are my KPIs? Those are short for key performance indicators. In my case, how many page views am I getting? How many conversions am I getting? What's my average shopping card value? What's my bounce rate? How many credit cards are coming in? What's my attrition rate? What's they pay once? Do you know that in my business, the attrition rate, once someone comes onto my platform and they go through the free trial, I lose X percent. After seven is it because the credit card doesn't go through, right? Do you know that once someone pays once, do you know what my attrition rate is? It's almost zero. How about that? No one leaves.
